content: Within the cytoplasm, mRNA functions as a transient yet information-rich intermediary, encoding the genetic template transcribed from DNA into a linear sequence of codons. Its 5’ cap and 3’ poly(A) tail, along with untranslated regions (UTRs), orchestrate interactions with ribosomes and regulatory proteins, modulating translation initiation and mRNA stability. The ribosome decodes the mRNA codons sequentially, facilitated by codon-anticodon pairing with charged tRNAs, ensuring fidelity in polypeptide elongation. Secondary structures and sequence motifs within the mRNA influence its accessibility and translational efficiency by affecting ribosomal scanning and recruitment of initiation factors. Additionally, post-transcriptional modifications such as methylation (e.g., m6A) dynamically regulate mRNA turnover and translational control, integrating cellular signaling cues. Thus, mRNA serves not merely as a static code but as a finely tuned substrate whose structural and chemical features coordinate spatiotemporal gene expression, coupling genetic information flow to the cellular environment’s demands.
